pattern: Once a pair of neighbors is too close in the alphabet, changing either one breaks that clash for free and can never help a clash you have not reached yet, so a single greedy sweep that skips ahead after every fix is optimal.
hints: Compare each character only to the one immediately before it -- 'too close' means equal or one letter apart in the alphabet either direction.
Scan left to right; the moment a neighboring pair clashes, count one change and assume that pair is now fixed for good.

After counting a fix between position i-1 and i, jump straight to position i+2 -- there is never a reason to also compare i and i+1 again, since you already resolved the earlier clash for the cost of one edit.

edge_inputs.description: single character, nothing adjacent to clash with
two letters at opposite ends of the alphabet, no clash
letters one apart at the very start of the alphabet
letters one apart at the very end of the alphabet
long run of a single repeated letter, testing the skip-by-two accounting
alternating pair of adjacent letters repeated, each neighboring pair clashes
no letter is within one of its neighbor anywhere in the string
a clash appears right after a run that was already resolved, checking the sweep does not over-fix

stress.description: random strings near maximum length drawn from the full lowercase alphabet
stress.generator_python:
import string
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    return [''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(n))]

stress.description: random strings drawn from a narrow 3-letter alphabet, forcing frequent almost-equal clashes
